key_insight: A machine-learning potential shows that Mg-related defects in SiC can alter thermal conductivity very differently depending on defect type and clustering, with some configurations scattering phonons far more strongly than others.
engineering_relevance: The work provides a quantitative atomistic tool for predicting and mitigating irradiation- and transmutation-driven heat-transport degradation in nuclear-grade SiC ceramics.
method: Trained MLIP4SiC-Mg on a large DFT dataset covering intrinsic defects, Mg defects, and Mg-defect complexes.
Combined Green-Kubo molecular dynamics with force-error correction to compute thermal conductivity.
Used a resistance-based dilute-defect treatment to compare isolated and clustered defect scattering.
evidence_signal: The corrected pristine 3C-SiC thermal conductivity is 421 W/(mK) at 300 K, and the model reproduces DFT energies, forces, phonons, and lattice thermal conductivities with near-DFT accuracy.
watch_item: Defect-induced thermal resistance is not strictly linear with concentration, so extrapolating from isolated-defect behavior to real irradiated microstructures may be misleading.
paper_title: Thermal Transport in SiC with Intrinsic Defects and Mg Transmutation Products
